metadata.summary: Use AI-generated research participants for zero-cost directional feedback during ideation, with mandatory validation gate before any product decision.

content.title: Synthetic Users
content.summary: Methodology for using AI-generated research participants to produce directional feedback during early ideation — covering persona simulation, interview analysis, and the mandatory gate of validating key findings with at least 3 real users before any product decision. Synthetic users are directional tools, never confirmatory evidence.

why.p: Real user research is slow and expensive. Synthetic users (Claude, GPT-4, Synthetic Users API, Viewpoints.ai) provide 0-cost, hours-turnaround directional feedback that stress-tests assumptions before committing to a study design. The critical failure mode is sycophancy: AI-generated users skew positive, lack nonverbal signals, and hallucinate domain knowledge in specialist fields. A validation gate to real users is non-negotiable.

list.item: Early ideation: generating directional feedback on concepts before recruiting real participants
Hypothesis generation: stress-testing assumptions with AI-simulated user archetypes
Rapid concept testing at zero research budget when timeline is days, not weeks
Producing illustrative failure scenarios for edge-case personas (low-literacy, non-native speakers)
Supplementing thin real-user datasets when recruitment would delay a critical decision
Go/no-go product decisions — synthetic feedback is directional only, never confirmatory
Demand forecasting or willingness-to-pay studies — AI is trained on text, not economic behavior
Sensitive population research (healthcare, legal, financial) — bias and hallucination risk is unacceptable
Any study output presented to investors or regulators as real user evidence
When you already have access to real users — synthetic users do not improve on real data

rule.statement: Use synthetic users for directional feedback only. Always follow synthetic research with at least 3 real user sessions before any product decision.
rule.rationale: Synthetic feedback is generated from text models trained on internet content, not real economic behavior or lived experience. It can surface hypotheses but cannot confirm them.

rule.statement: Explicitly label all synthetic research findings as "Synthetic research — directional only, not validated" in every research report.
rule.rationale: Unlabeled synthetic findings create false confidence; stakeholders who cannot distinguish synthetic from real data make decisions based on AI-generated bias, not user reality.

rule.statement: Never use synthetic users for go/no-go product decisions, demand forecasting, willingness-to-pay studies, or research presented to investors or regulators as real user evidence.
rule.rationale: These use cases require confirmatory evidence; synthetic users provide directional signal only and are trained on text, not economic behavior or regulatory-grade user observation.

example.code:
Stage 1 — Synthetic (fast, cheap, directional): → Define personas from real behavioral data (analytics, CRM) → Run 5+ synthetic participants per concept → Extract themes; flag sycophantic responses as low-confidence → Produce hypotheses for real-user validation
Stage 2 — Real users (slower, confirmatory): → Validate top 3 synthetic findings with at least 3 real user sessions → Note where real user responses diverge from synthetic → Use divergence as indicator of AI bias or hallucination
Decision gate: product decisions require Stage 2 completion.

rule.statement: Define personas from real behavioral data (analytics segments, CRM attributes), not from demographic stereotypes.
rule.rationale: Stereotype-based personas amplify AI bias; behavioral anchors (actual usage patterns, observed pain points) produce more reliable synthetic simulation.

rule.statement: Retire synthetic personas after 30 days or major product changes.
rule.rationale: Stale personas drift from current user reality as the product evolves; outdated personas produce directional signals pointing in the wrong direction.

rule.statement: Cross-validate with two different AI providers (Claude + GPT-4). Where outputs diverge, treat findings as low-confidence.
rule.rationale: Divergence between providers signals that the response is model-dependent, not user-representative; these findings require real-user validation before acting on.

antipattern.description: Running a single synthetic participant and treating the output as representative.
antipattern.reason: Single-participant synthetic outputs have no statistical meaning; run at least 5 per concept for any directional signal.

antipattern.description: Using synthetic users for specialist domain research (medicine, law, finance) without explicit bias warnings.
antipattern.reason: Synthetic users in specialist fields hallucinate plausible-sounding domain knowledge; outputs cannot be used to validate domain-specific product decisions.

rule.statement: Synthetic feedback skews positive — AI-generated users are prone to sycophantic responses that favor the described product.
rule.rationale: Training data bias toward positive sentiment means synthetic users underreport skepticism and negative reactions; expect 15–20% fewer pain points than real users report.

rule.statement: No nonverbal data: body language, tone, hesitation, confusion signals are absent.
rule.rationale: Synthetic simulation is text-only; the loss of behavioral cues (e.g., "user paused for 3 seconds") removes critical insight markers.

rule.statement: Persona consistency breaks down across long sessions; AI forgets constraints mid-interview.
rule.rationale: Long transcripts (90+ minutes of simulated interview) cause the AI to drift from persona definition as context window fills; shorter sessions (5–10 questions) maintain fidelity.

rule.statement: No industry standard for synthetic user quality; outputs from Synthetic Users, Aaru, and Viewpoints.ai are not comparable.
rule.rationale: Each provider uses different base models, prompting strategies, and validation; synthesized findings cannot be aggregated across providers without explicit methodology notes.

rule.statement: Hallucinated domain knowledge: synthetic users in specialist fields (medicine, law, finance) make up plausible-sounding but wrong behaviors.
rule.rationale: Domain expert simulants require either fine-tuned models or explicit constraints in prompts; off-the-shelf models fail domain-specific validation checks.

rule.statement: Reproducing results is impossible without pinning exact model version and temperature.
rule.rationale: Model updates and temperature variation produce different synthetic user outputs for identical personas; research reproducibility requires exact version tracking.

section.name: simulation-workflow
section.p: Claude subagents can simulate synthetic user interviews directly: given a persona definition and a discussion guide, the agent responds as the simulated user across multiple questions. A second agent (researcher role) then analyzes the synthetic interview transcripts for themes. The loop is: human defines persona + study → agent plays user → agent analyzes → human validates key findings with at least 3 real users. Never skip the real-user validation gate.

list.item: sonnet — synthetic user simulation (requires nuanced persona adherence), interview analysis, theme extraction
haiku — persona attribute generation, quantitative response scoring (Likert-scale surveys at scale)

rule.statement: Use temperature 0.7–0.9 for synthetic user simulation. Temperature 0 makes responses too consistent — all personas converge to the same answers.
rule.rationale: Realistic variation between synthetic users requires non-zero temperature; without it, running 5 personas produces the same output 5 times, which is not directionally useful.

antipattern.description: Agent playing a synthetic user breaks character when probed on technical details — answers as AI, not as persona.
antipattern.reason: LLM simulation degrades under probing; deep technical questions reveal the AI substrate; these responses are not valid as synthetic user data.

antipattern.description: Synthetic users display implausible product knowledge in simulation.
antipattern.reason: AI models are trained on product descriptions on the internet; synthetic personas know more about your product than a real user would. Prime explicitly: instruct the persona to have limited product awareness.

antipattern.description: Agent-analyzed themes from synthetic interviews lack evidence quotes.
antipattern.reason: Synthetic themes have double hallucination risk (simulation + analysis); require evidence quotes for every extracted theme to surface fabrication.

list.item: anthropic Python SDK — Run synthetic user simulations directly via Claude API (pip install anthropic)
openai Python SDK — Run synthetic user simulations via GPT-4 for cross-model validation (pip install openai)
faker — Generate realistic persona attribute datasets (names, locations, jobs) (pip install faker)
Synthetic Users (syntheticusers.com) — SaaS, REST API for persona-based interview simulation
Viewpoints.ai — SaaS, Rapid concept validation; quantitative + qualitative output
Aaru — SaaS, Scenario testing; API in beta; best for predictive analysis
SyntheticAudience.ai — SaaS, Quantitative survey simulation at scale
Qualtrics Edge Audience — SaaS, Modeled panels; requires Qualtrics subscription + sales (no public API)
UserIntelligence — SaaS, Simulation engine; no public API

example.title: Synthetic User Simulation
example.code: <system> You are a synthetic research participant. Persona: - Name: Maya, 34, marketing manager - Tech comfort: medium (uses Slack, Notion, G-Suite daily; avoids CLI tools) - Goal: reduce time spent on weekly reporting - Pain: current tool requires manual CSV exports Respond only as Maya. Stay in character. Do not be generically positive. If a question is unclear to Maya, say so. If Maya would not use the product, say so. </system> <human>What is your first reaction when you see this onboarding screen? [describe screen]</human>

example.title: Theme Analysis with Confidence Flags
example.code: Analyze these 5 synthetic interview transcripts [paste]. Extract: (1) top 3 recurring pain points with evidence quotes, (2) 2 positive reactions, (3) 2 areas where synthetic users gave suspiciously consistent or positive responses (flag as low-confidence). Output structured report with confidence level per finding.

list.item: Define personas from real behavioral data (analytics segments, CRM attributes) not from stereotypes — garbage persona in, garbage output
Run at least 5 synthetic participants per concept; single-participant outputs are not meaningful
Flag synthetic findings explicitly in research reports: "Synthetic research — directional only, not validated"
Always follow synthetic research with at least 3 real user sessions before any product decision
Cross-validate with two different AI providers (Claude + GPT-4) and note where outputs diverge — divergence signals low-confidence areas
Retire synthetic personas after 30 days or major product changes; stale personas drift from current user reality

template.purpose: Run multi-persona synthetic interview panel via Claude API
System prompt for synthetic user interview simulation
Prompt for extracting themes from synthetic interview transcripts

see-also.link: ACM CHI 2023: LLMs as user surrogates
